title: Saturday Questions: Backtracking in Python - Replies
description: Solutions and explanations for beginner to advanced backtracking questions in Python.

replies.title: Print All Binary Strings of Length n
replies.explanation: At every position, we have two choices: put 0 or put 1. We keep building the string until its length becomes n.
replies.keyIdea: Choice: 0 or 1. Explore both. No special undo is needed because strings are immutable.
replies.code:
def generate_binary(n):
    result = []

    def backtrack(path):
        if len(path) == n:
            result.append(path)
            return

        backtrack(path + "0")
        backtrack(path + "1")

    backtrack("")
    return result

print(generate_binary(3))
replies.complexity: Time: O(2^n), Space: O(n) recursion depth excluding output.

replies.title: Print All Subsets of a List
replies.explanation: For each element, we decide whether to skip it or include it. When we reach the end of the list, we save the current subset.
replies.code:
def subsets(arr):
    result = []

    def backtrack(index, path):
        if index == len(arr):
            result.append(path[:])
            return

        # choice 1: skip current element
        backtrack(index + 1, path)

        # choice 2: include current element
        path.append(arr[index])
        backtrack(index + 1, path)
        path.pop()

    backtrack(0, [])
    return result

print(subsets([1, 2, 3]))
replies.complexity: Time: O(2^n), Space: O(n) recursion depth excluding output.

replies.title: Print All Permutations of a String
replies.explanation: A permutation uses every character exactly once. We choose an unused character, mark it as used, explore further, and then unmark it.
replies.complexity: Time: O(n!), Space: O(n) recursion depth excluding output.

replies.title: Generate Valid Parentheses
replies.explanation: A valid parentheses string never has more closing brackets than opening brackets. We add '(' if we still can. We add ')' only if it will not break the balance.
replies.keyIdea: Open count must be less than n. Close count must be less than open count.
replies.complexity: Time: related to Catalan numbers, Space: O(n) recursion depth excluding output.

replies.title: Find All Paths in a Maze
replies.explanation: Starting from the top-left cell, we try moving right and down. If we reach the bottom-right cell, we save the path.
replies.keyIdea: From each cell, try R and D if the move remains inside the grid.
replies.complexity: Time: exponential in path length, Space: O(rows + cols) recursion depth excluding output.

replies.title: Find Combinations That Sum to Target
replies.explanation: We keep choosing numbers as long as the total does not exceed the target. Because a number may be reused, the next recursive call starts from the same index.
replies.keyIdea: Use start index to avoid duplicate combinations. Reuse is allowed, so pass i again.
replies.complexity: Time: exponential, Space: O(target / smallest candidate) recursion depth excluding output.

replies.title: Solve the N-Queens Problem
replies.explanation: We place one queen in each row. Before placing a queen, we check whether the column and diagonals are safe.
replies.keyIdea: A queen attacks vertically and diagonally. Use sets to track used columns and diagonals.
replies.code:
def solve_n_queens(n):
    result = []
    board = [["."] * n for _ in range(n)]

    cols = set()
    diag1 = set()  # row - col
    diag2 = set()  # row + col

    def backtrack(row):
        if row == n:
            result.append(["".join(r) for r in board])
            return

        for col in range(n):
            if col in cols or (row - col) in diag1 or (row + col) in diag2:
                continue

            board[row][col] = "Q"
            cols.add(col)
            diag1.add(row - col)
            diag2.add(row + col)

            backtrack(row + 1)

            board[row][col] = "."
            cols.remove(col)
            diag1.remove(row - col)
            diag2.remove(row + col)

    backtrack(0)
    return result

for solution in solve_n_queens(4):
    for row in solution:
        print(row)
    print()
replies.complexity: Time: approximately O(n!), Space: O(n^2) for board and O(n) for tracking sets.

replies.title: Search a Word in a Grid
replies.explanation: We try to match the word character by character. From each matching cell, we explore four directions. A cell cannot be reused in the same path.
replies.keyIdea: Mark the current cell as visited, explore, then unmark it.
replies.complexity: Time: O(rows * cols * 4^word length), Space: O(word length) recursion depth.

replies.title: Rat in a Maze with Blocked Cells
replies.explanation: The rat can move in four directions. We avoid blocked cells, already visited cells, and cells outside the maze. When we reach the destination, we save the path.
replies.keyIdea: Mark visited before exploring and unmark while returning.
replies.code:
def rat_in_maze(maze):
    n = len(maze)
    result = []
    visited = [[False] * n for _ in range(n)]

    directions = [
        (1, 0, "D"),
        (0, -1, "L"),
        (0, 1, "R"),
        (-1, 0, "U")
    ]

    def backtrack(r, c, path):
        if r < 0 or r >= n or c < 0 or c >= n:
            return

        if maze[r][c] == 0 or visited[r][c]:
            return

        if r == n - 1 and c == n - 1:
            result.append(path)
            return

        visited[r][c] = True

        for dr, dc, move in directions:
            backtrack(r + dr, c + dc, path + move)

        visited[r][c] = False

    if maze[0][0] == 1:
        backtrack(0, 0, "")

    return result

maze = [
    [1, 0, 0, 0],
    [1, 1, 0, 1],
    [1, 1, 0, 0],
    [0, 1, 1, 1]
]

print(rat_in_maze(maze))
replies.complexity: Time: O(4^(n*n)) in the worst case, Space: O(n*n).

replies.title: Solve Sudoku Using Backtracking
replies.explanation: We find an empty cell and try digits from 1 to 9. If a digit is valid in its row, column, and 3 x 3 box, we place it and continue. If later it fails, we undo the digit.
replies.keyIdea: Try digit, check safety, recurse, undo if needed.
replies.complexity: Time: O(9^empty cells) in the worst case, Space: O(empty cells) recursion depth.
